name: Khazari
description:
The Khazari evolved on Drakenforge, a brutal volcanic world of crushing gravity and rivers of molten basalt, where the weak were consumed by the planet long before predators got the chance. Thick-scaled, dense-boned, and built like siege engines, the Khazari spent their early millennia not fleeing from fire but walking into it. They are the galaxy's supreme metallurgists. While other species discovered smelting as a breakthrough, the Khazari discovered it as a birthright — their earliest tools were forged in lava flows, hammered on anvils of cooled obsidian. To be Khazari is to shape the world with your hands, and a Khazari who cannot forge is not truly alive.

They came to the stars because it was inevitable. The Khazari do not expand out of fear or curiosity — they expand because expansion is what a forge-species does. New worlds are new anvils. New star systems are new furnaces. The Khazari word for colonisation, 'vrekash', translates literally as 'to claim a forge.' Their word for diplomacy, 'drenok-vash', translates roughly as 'shouting last.' This is not stupidity. The Khazari understand negotiation perfectly well — they simply believe that the species willing to keep arguing the longest deserves the outcome. Stubbornness is not a flaw in Khazari culture. It is the highest civic virtue.

Beneath the blunt exterior lies a civilisation of surprising depth. Khazari honour is not merely about combat prowess — it is about craftsmanship. A master shipwright commands more respect than a victorious admiral, because the admiral's victory was only possible because of the shipwright's hull. Every Khazari warship is a signed work. Somewhere in the superstructure, etched in heat-resistant alloy, is the name of the forge-clan that built it. To destroy a Khazari vessel is to destroy a work of art, and the Khazari will remember. They keep grudges the way other species keep records — meticulously, permanently, and with great attention to detail.

The great tension within Khazari society is between the Forge-Lords, who believe the species' destiny lies in building — ever greater works, ever more magnificent constructions, megastructures that dwarf planets — and the War-Speakers, who believe destiny lies in conquest. The Forge-Lords argue that war is merely a means to acquire new resources for building. The War-Speakers counter that building is merely a means to wage better wars. This argument has been running for six thousand years, and both sides consider it a point of honour that neither has yielded. In practice, the Khazari do both with equal enthusiasm, which makes them one of the most dangerous species in the galaxy — enemies who can outproduce you AND outfight you.
